Projects

01

Hardware · PCB Design · Audio Engineering · CMPE 110 — Fall 2025

2-Watt Stereo Audio Amplifier

Designed, simulated, and physically manufactured a dual-channel stereo audio amplifier PCB from scratch. The project covered the complete engineering lifecycle — from circuit theory and simulation in LTSpice, through schematic capture and multi-layer PCB layout in KiCad, to through-hole soldering and oscilloscope verification. The board successfully amplified stereo audio at 2 watts per channel, verified clean output at 15kHz, 20kHz, and 25kHz on a Tektronix DPO 3032 oscilloscope.

KiCad 9.0LTSpiceLM386 Op-AmpGerber / Drill FilesThrough-hole SolderingOscilloscope TestingBOM ExportERC Verification

Step 1 — Design & Simulation (LTSpice)

Circuit modeled in LTSpice using the LM386 op-amp before touching KiCad. Gain network built with specific capacitor values, potentiometer added as volume control. Waveform simulation confirmed clean amplification with no clipping.

Fig 1 — LTSpice simulation: dual-channel LM386 configuration with gain network and volume control

Step 2 — Schematic Capture (KiCad)

Circuit recreated in KiCad's Schematic Editor. Left and right channels fully wired with power, filtering caps, and bypass caps. ERC run to completion — 0 errors, 0 warnings.

ERC Result

0 errors · 0 warnings — all pin connections verified, power flags confirmed, schematic locked in.

Fig 2 — Final KiCad schematic: stereo amplifier with power switch, LED indicator, and dual LM386 stages

Step 3 — Footprint Assignment

Schematic symbols mapped to real-world physical packages. LM386 assigned DIP-8_W7.62mm. Capacitors measured in lab and matched to correct CP_Radial footprints. Pin numbers verified against datasheet.

Fig 3 — Footprint assignment: all 26 components mapped from symbols to physical package sizes

Step 4 — PCB Layout: Place & Route

Most challenging phase of the project. Two-layer routing with manual trace placement. Initial rat's nest placement required multiple rearrangements. Final layout achieved with all nets routed and no DRC violations.

01

Layer management

First time working with two copper layers. Had to learn when to use vias to jump between F.Cu and B.Cu without creating shorts.

02

The rat's nest

Initial placement created a web of crossing connections. Components rearranged multiple times to untangle all nets.

03

Routing conflicts

Traces blocked each other across the board. Required systematic trial and error to find paths that worked for every net simultaneously.

Fig 4 — Final PCB layout: all copper traces routed across two layers, no DRC violations

Fig 5 — 3D render: component placement and spacing verified pre-manufacturing

Step 5 — PCB Assembly & Soldering

All components — LM386 chips, electrolytic caps, resistors, potentiometers, and power LED — soldered through-hole. Multiple rounds of rework required to fix bridges and cold joints. Board fully assembled with all components in place.

Fig 6 — Physical board: bare PCB front (left), copper back layer (center), fully assembled after soldering (right)

Step 6 — Testing & Verification

9V power supply connected. LED lit immediately confirming correct VCC/GND rails. Stereo audio input applied and output measured on Tektronix DPO 3032. Clean sinusoidal output confirmed at all three test frequencies.

Power test

LED lit immediately on 9V power-up. VCC and GND rails confirmed correct.

Audio output

Stereo amplified audio confirmed. Clean signal with no clipping on oscilloscope.

Frequency range

Clean waveforms verified at 15kHz, 20kHz, and 25kHz on Tektronix DPO 3032.

Fig 7 — Tektronix DPO 3032: clean amplified output at 15kHz, 20kHz, and 25kHz

Step 7 — Manufacturing File Generation

Gerber files exported for all layers: F.Cu, B.Cu, F.Mask, B.Mask, F.SilkS, Edge.Cuts. Drill files generated for CNC through-hole coordinates. Full BOM exported as CSV. All files verified in GerbView before submission.

Fig 8 — Gerber viewer: trace continuity and drill hole alignment verified

Fig 9 — Bill of materials: full component list exported as CSV for manufacturing

Project Outcome

Complete PCB lifecycle from theory to physical product. Confirmed stereo audio amplification at 2W per channel with clean output verified across 15–25kHz frequency range.

02

Hardware · RF Circuits · Signal Analysis · EE 97 — Fall 2024

RF Resonant Circuit & Frequency Tuning

Built and analyzed an RF resonant LC circuit on a breadboard using an SJSU EE97 coil module. The circuit was tuned across frequencies by adjusting the resonant point, with output behavior measured and analyzed on a Tektronix DPO 3012 oscilloscope. Observed resonant frequency behavior at approximately 3.1–3.15 MHz with peak-to-peak voltages ranging from 524mV to 8.56V across different tuning configurations. Developed hands-on understanding of LC resonance, RF signal behavior, and high-frequency oscilloscope measurement at the MHz range.

LC Resonant CircuitRF InductorsBreadboard PrototypingOscilloscope AnalysisFrequency Tuning3.1MHz Signal Range

Circuit Setup & Breadboard Wiring

RF inductor coil module (SJSU EE97) wired into the breadboard alongside supporting passive components. Circuit connected to a function generator as the signal source with oscilloscope probes on the output nodes to capture the signal at each tuned frequency.

Circuit overview: SJSU RF coil module wired on breadboard

Full breadboard wiring with power and probe connections

Circuit from above showing complete probe setup

Oscilloscope Measurements

Output signal captured at multiple tuning points. Frequency locked in at approximately 3.1–3.15 MHz across all measurements. Peak-to-peak amplitude varied significantly with tuning — from 524mV at the input level up to 8.56V at resonance peak — demonstrating the characteristic voltage amplification behavior of a resonant LC circuit near its resonant frequency.

Resonant frequency

Signal locked at 3.104–3.149 MHz across all tuning configurations.

Peak output

8.56V peak-to-peak at resonance — demonstrating LC voltage amplification.

Input signal

524mV input rising to 544mV — confirmed gain through resonant circuit.

Oscilloscope: 524mV P-P at 3.141 MHz — input level measurement

Oscilloscope: 8.56V P-P at 3.104 MHz — peak resonance output

Oscilloscope: 544mV P-P at 3.137 MHz — secondary tuning point

Full Stack · Healthcare · Live Product

Sugar Scout

Built and actively maintain a full-stack web platform dedicated to helping people understand and manage diabetes. Provides accessible, well-researched health information to underserved communities. Designed, developed, and deployed independently — live and in active use.

04

Software · C++ · Systems Programming

ASCII First Person Shooter

Built a first-person shooter rendered entirely in ASCII characters through the Windows command prompt. Implemented screen buffers, raycasting-style map rendering, time differentials, directional key movement, and collision detection — all in raw C++. No graphics library, no engine — just the terminal and math.

C++Screen BuffersRaycastingCollision DetectionTime Differential
